meta.title: 1.2 The Anatomy of a Page
meta.description: Understand the major sections of an HTML document — head, body, and semantic elements — and what goes where.

blocks.prompt: You know that HTML tags label content. But a full webpage has a lot more going on — a title in the browser tab, styles that control how it looks, and the visible content itself. Where do you think all of those different things live inside the HTML?

blocks.content:
## The skeleton of every webpage Every HTML page follows the same basic structure, no matter how simple or complex: ```html My Page Title

Hello, world!

This is visible content.

``` Let's walk through each part. ## The `` tag: the wrapper The `` tag wraps everything. It is the outermost container — like the cover of a book that holds all the pages inside. ## The ``: behind the scenes The `` section contains information **about** the page, but nothing the user sees directly on the page itself. Think of it as the backstage area: - **``** — The text that appears in the browser tab. Not on the page itself, just the tab. - **`<link>`** — Connects stylesheets (CSS files) that control how the page looks. - **`<meta>`** — Metadata like character encoding, description for search engines, and viewport settings for mobile. - **`<script>`** — Can load JavaScript files (though scripts can also go at the end of the body). If you have ever seen a browser tab that says "Untitled Document" or just shows the file path, it means the `<title>` tag is missing or empty in the `<head>`. ## The `<body>`: what users see The `<body>` section contains everything that is visible on the page — all the headings, paragraphs, images, buttons, and links. If it shows up on screen, it lives in the `<body>`. ## Semantic elements: giving the body structure Inside the `<body>`, you could technically put everything in `<div>` tags and the page would look fine. But modern HTML has **semantic elements** — tags that describe the *role* of the content, not just that it is a container: ```html <body> <header> <nav> <a href="/">Home</a> <a href="/about">About</a> </nav> </header> <main> <article> <h1>How to Make Sourdough</h1> <p>Start with a good starter...</p> </article> </main> <footer> <p>Copyright 2025 My Bakery</p> </footer> </body> ``` Here is what each one means: - **`<header>`** — The top section of the page (logo, navigation, banner) - **`<nav>`** — A navigation area with links - **`<main>`** — The primary content of the page - **`<article>`** — A self-contained piece of content (blog post, news story, product listing) - **`<section>`** — A thematic grouping of content within the page - **`<footer>`** — The bottom section (copyright, contact info, legal links) ## Why semantic elements matter You might wonder: if `<div>` and `<main>` look the same on screen, why bother? Two big reasons: 1. **Accessibility.** Screen readers use semantic tags to help visually impaired users navigate. A screen reader can jump straight to `<main>` or list all the `<nav>` links. If everything is a `<div>`, the screen reader has no idea what is what. 2. **Search engines.** Google reads semantic tags to understand page structure, which affects how your content ranks and appears in search results. When you see a page built entirely with `<div>` tags and no semantic elements, it is a red flag — not because the page will look broken, but because it will be harder for machines (screen readers, search engines, AI tools) to understand.

blocks.question: On a scale of 1-5, how confident are you that you could look at an HTML file and identify which parts are in the head (not visible) versus the body (visible)?
blocks.answer: If you rated yourself 3 or higher, great. The key distinction is simple: <head> is metadata about the page (title, styles, scripts), <body> is the visible content. Everything the user sees on the page is in the body.

problems.question:
Where does the text inside the `<title>` tag appear?

```html
<head>
  <title>Our Company - Home</title>
</head>
```
options: A) In the browser tab (and search engine results), not on the page itself
B) As a large heading at the top of the visible page
C) As small text in the footer of the page
problems.explanation: The <title> tag lives in the <head>, which is the behind-the-scenes section. Its text shows up in the browser tab and in search engine results, but it does not appear anywhere on the visible page.

problems.question:
A tester reports that a screen reader cannot navigate this page — it just reads everything as a flat wall of text with no sections. What is the most likely issue? ```html
...
...
...
```
options: A) The CSS classes are not descriptive enough
B) The page uses only <div> tags instead of semantic elements like <header>, <nav>, <main>, and <footer>
C) The page is missing a <title> tag
problems.explanation: Screen readers rely on semantic HTML elements to understand page structure. When everything is a <div>, the screen reader cannot tell what is navigation, what is the main content, and what is the footer. Replacing those <div> tags with <header>, <nav>, <main>, and <footer> would fix the navigation issue.

problems.question:
A client says: "The browser tab just says 'Untitled Document' instead of our company name." You view the source and see this: ```html

Acme Corp

Welcome to our website.

``` What needs to be fixed?
options: A) The <h1> tag should be moved into the <head>
B) A <title>Acme Corp</title> tag needs to be added inside the <head>
C) The <link> tag is in the wrong place and is overriding the title
problems.explanation: The browser tab text comes from the <title> tag inside the <head>. This page has a <head> section but no <title> tag in it, so the browser shows a default like "Untitled Document." Adding <title>Acme Corp</title> inside the <head> fixes it. The <h1> is for visible page content — it does not affect the browser tab.

blocks.prompt: Explain the difference between the <head> and <body> sections of an HTML page. What kind of things go in each one, and why does it matter?

blocks.content: Every HTML page has the same skeleton: <html> wraps everything, <head> holds behind-the-scenes metadata (title, styles, scripts), and <body> holds the visible content. Inside the body, semantic elements like <header>, <nav>, <main>, and <footer> give structure that screen readers and search engines depend on.

blocks.prompt: You can now read the structure of a page from top to bottom. But pages are not just headings and paragraphs — they have links you can click, images you can see, and lists of items. Next, we will cover the most common HTML elements you will encounter in the wild.

reviewQuestions.question: In an HTML page, the <head> element contains:
options: The visible content users see on the page
Behind-the-scenes metadata like the page title, styles, and scripts
reviewQuestions.explanation: The <head> holds metadata (title, linked stylesheets, scripts) that the browser needs but users don't see directly. The <body> holds visible content.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Right! The head is behind the scenes, the body is what you see.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The <head> contains metadata like the page title, styles, and scripts. The <body> holds all the visible content.

reviewQuestions.question: Which HTML element contains everything visible on a webpage?
reviewQuestions.explanation: The <body> element contains all the visible content — text, images, buttons, and everything else users see and interact with.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Correct! Everything you see lives inside the <body> tag.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The <body> element holds all visible content. The <head> is for metadata, and <title> just sets the browser tab text.

reviewQuestions.question: Semantic HTML elements like <header>, <nav>, <main>, and <footer> are useful because they:
options: Give structure that screen readers and search engines can understand
reviewQuestions.explanation: Semantic elements describe the purpose of content, helping screen readers navigate for accessibility and helping search engines understand page structure.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Well done! Semantic HTML is about meaning, not just appearance.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Semantic elements describe what content is for (navigation, main content, footer), helping screen readers and search engines understand the page.
